title: Differentiable rendering with perturbed optimizers

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable renderer by introducing perturbed optimizers. The paper re-writes the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps to heaven side functions and relaxes them to be differentiable by adding noise. 
The author shows that previous work, especially softras, can be treated as a special case of the perturbed renderer with specific prior selections. The authors also demonstrated in several experiments they achieve better or comparable results than the state of the art.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths

1) Differentiable reformulation
This paper treats rasterization as a linear programing problem.  It extends the [4] to the rendering pipeline and relaxes the non-differentiable rendering to be differentiable by introducing perturbed optimizers. It provides a new differentiable renderer from a new perspective.

2) Better performance
The authors show AS and VR are very useful in optimization, for both perturbed renderer and softras. In the 6D pose comparison, the results are better than softras.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
There are several weak points of this paper.

1) The paper is not self-contained.  I have a hard time understanding each equation. While the authors largely rely on [4], it makes readers hard to follow without knowing [4]. Moreover, the notations in this paper are very vague.   E.g. line 66 it should be j \in [1,.., m]?  line 76 z should be depth order?  If it is depth, how do you compute <z, y>?  line 88, what's v?  The paper writing definitely needs to be improved.


2) Relation of softras and noise types.  The authors show that softras can be treated as the special case of the perturbed renderer. Softras has some hyperparameters which are hard to explain but in the new renderer, all the parameters have clear noise intensity meaning.  While this is very good, but the paper lacks further analysis of which noise should we use. E.g. in table 1, it seems Gaussian noise is the best. But could it be generalized to other cases? 


3) Comparison.  In line 214 the authors said, "Unless stated, the results for the other renderers are extracted from their original publications, as we were not able to get access to their implementations."  I am a little confused about this.  Clearly, N3mr, softras, dibr released their renderers. Thus, why not simply replace the renderer from perturbed renderer to new renderers and compare the score?  Directly copying scores from others' papers may have the wrong comparison. While all the methods use shapenet, the rendered images could vary from lighting to camera views. I would highly recommend the authors redo the comparison.


4)  The authors assume each pixel will be influenced by all the triangles. For each pixel, it renders the influences of all the triangles and combines them together(Eq 3).  I wonder what's the rendering speed of the perturbed renderer? Theoretically, such design plus the MC sampling will make the renderer very time-consuming.  The authors should show the rendering speed comparison.


5) Following the last concern.  Generally speaking, rasterization accelerates the rendering speed by assuming each pixel is influenced by the frontest triangle.  On the other side, MC is always used in ray tracing to compute multiple bounces. The authors introduce MC in rasterization, which seems to adopt the weak points of two rendering methods.   I wonder did the authors consider extending it to RT?

Post rebuttal:

The authors addressed my concerns and I will raise the score to be marginally above the threshold and be happy to see this paper get accepted.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new differentiable rendering method, differentiable perturbed renderers,  wherein the gradients of the rasterization step are approximated using perturbed optimizers. They implement this change on top of the SoftRas method and demonstrate that it leads to better pose estimation, and on par performance with state of the art methods in unsupervised single image 3d object reconstruction.
reviews.main_review:
This is a strong paper. It is written clearly, and the method is explained properly. Perturbed optimizers seem to be a new way for tackling optimization over discrete operations in deep learning, and their application to rasterization is well motivated and appropriate. The theory required for applying them to this new setting seems right, and is well explained. The results are also adequate for the claims made. It would have been nice to see higher performance on the reconstruction task, though diff rendering is a relatively new and so highlighting new avenues to explore , such as your proposed perturbed optimizers,  is important, and so I do not believe it is necessary.

 I would say one nice set of experiments to see would be optimization over the same set of parameters as in the DIB-R  paper Figure 2. ( camera position, texture , vertex positions, etc. ), with qualitative and quantitative comparisons to DIB-R and SoftRas. Having this would go a long was to increasing my score, and I believe would sell you paper quite a bit better. The pose estimation seems quite a bit easier than optimizing for texture and vertex position for example, and it would be nice to see how you method fairs, even if the performance is only on par or worse.

reviews.text:
Recommendation: 7 (Good paper, accept)

Summary
This paper presents a clear and well-motivated application of perturbed optimizers to differentiable rasterization. By approximating gradients through the rasterization step via perturbations, the method offers a principled way to handle the discrete operations inherent in rendering. The approach is implemented on top of SoftRas and evaluated on two tasks: 6D pose estimation and unsupervised single-image 3D object reconstruction. Theoretical justification is sound and well explained, and the empirical results support the main claims—particularly the improvements in pose estimation—while showing on-par performance with state-of-the-art methods for reconstruction.

Strengths
- The paper is clearly written, with the method and theory carefully explained.
- Using perturbed optimizers for discrete operations is novel and well suited to rasterization; the motivation is compelling and appropriate for the setting.
- The theoretical treatment appears correct and is well communicated.
- Results are adequate relative to the claims, with notable gains in pose estimation performance.
- Reconstruction performance is competitive with state-of-the-art in unsupervised single-image 3D reconstruction.

Weaknesses
- Reconstruction performance, while competitive, could be stronger.
- The experimental scope is limited with respect to optimizing a broader set of parameters (e.g., camera position, texture, vertex positions) as done in DIB-R.
- As pose estimation is generally easier than texture and vertex optimization, it remains unclear how the proposed method performs on these harder optimization problems.

Suggestions for improvement
- Add experiments that optimize the same set of parameters as in DIB-R Figure 2, including camera position, texture, and vertex positions.
- Provide both qualitative and quantitative comparisons to DIB-R and SoftRas on these parameter-optimization tasks.
- Include results even if they are on par or worse than baselines, to more clearly position the method’s strengths and limitations on harder optimization problems.

Overall assessment
This is a strong and clearly presented paper with a well-justified theoretical foundation and solid empirical support for its central claims. The improvements in pose estimation are convincing, and reconstruction results are competitive. The work would be significantly strengthened by expanding the experimental evaluation to harder parameter-optimization settings aligned with DIB-R and by providing direct comparisons on those tasks. Despite this limitation, the contribution is meaningful and timely. I recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary:
This paper presents a method for converting a standard non-differentiable rasterization-based renderer into a differentiable counterpart using recent advances in the perturbed optimizer. The main contribution lies in the generic nature provided by the theoretical formulation, where some specifically chosen priors in the proposed framework can retrieve recent state-of-the-art differentiable renderers, e.g. SoftRas. 
As the proposed approach mainly relies on a Monte-Carlo estimator for generating the smoothing parameters. Variance-reduction mechanism and sensitivity analysis are also incorporated into the proposed framework to ensure the robustness of the approximate renderer. Experiments on pose estimation and single-view reconstruction are conducted for comparing the performance of the proposed method and previous state-of-the-art approaches.
reviews.main_review:
Pros:

- By introducing the perturbed optimizer to analyzing the non-differentiability of standard rasterization-based rendering pipeline provides new insight into constructing a generic differentiable renderer. The perturbed differentiable rendering can also be implemented on top of existing renderers without major modifications, which makes this approach versatile and a potential performance booster for existing DR techniques.

Cons:

- In terms of technical novelty, it seems to be a direct application of perturbed optimizer to differentiable rendering. Though it requires some efforts (such as the variance reduction and sensitivity analysis) to adapt the existing theory to this specific application, it is not very challenging as there are existing protocols to follow.

- Evaluation-wise, in the task of single-view reconstruction, though the proposed method can achieve comparable performance compared to the state-of-the-art approaches, it still fails to outperform DIB-R in mean accuracy and also outperformed by SoftRas in many categories, such as the Dresser, Car, etc. The 3D pose estimation is only evaluated on a simple cube which cannot provide a very comprehensive measurement of the proposed approach.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Main Review:
Pros:

reviews.text:
Summary
This paper proposes a generic differentiable rendering framework built on perturbed optimizers. The core idea is to handle rasterization non-differentiabilities via a Monte Carlo estimator for smoothing parameters, augmented with variance-reduction techniques and sensitivity analysis to improve robustness. The framework is designed to be easily layered on top of existing renderers and can, under specific priors, recover existing differentiable renderers such as SoftRas. The empirical evaluation shows competitive but mixed results, and the discussion of limitations and societal impact is included.

Strengths
- Conceptual clarity and generality: Using perturbed optimizers to reason about and mitigate non-differentiability in rasterization provides an insightful lens and yields a framework that can subsume prior approaches (e.g., retrieving SoftRas via particular priors).
- Practicality: The method can be integrated with existing renderers without major changes, making it a potentially valuable, drop-in tool for current differentiable rendering pipelines.
- Robustness: The reliance on Monte Carlo estimation is complemented by variance-reduction mechanisms and sensitivity analysis, which are thoughtfully incorporated to stabilize training.

Weaknesses
- Limited technical novelty: The contribution reads primarily as a direct application of perturbed optimizer theory to differentiable rendering, with established protocols guiding much of the design.
- Mixed empirical gains: In single-view reconstruction, the method is competitive but does not consistently improve over strong baselines; it fails to surpass DIB-R on mean accuracy and is outperformed by SoftRas in several categories (notably Dresser and Car).
- Narrow pose evaluation: Pose estimation is demonstrated only on a simple cube, which is insufficient to convincingly establish the method’s effectiveness for broader pose estimation tasks.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Expand the pose estimation evaluation beyond a simple cube to provide a more comprehensive and convincing assessment.
- Strengthen single-view reconstruction results to surpass DIB-R and SoftRas across more categories, or provide clearer analysis of when and why the proposed approach should be expected to excel.
- Clarify the specific aspects of novelty beyond the straightforward application of perturbed optimizers, and highlight any unique insights or design choices that are essential to making the framework practical and robust.

Overall Assessment
The paper presents a coherent and generic framework with practical appeal, and the robustness mechanisms are well-motivated. However, the perception of limited novelty and the breadth and strength of the empirical evaluation temper the impact. On balance, I find the contribution marginally positive, with clear potential value to the differentiable rendering community if the evaluation and positioning can be strengthened. Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold).

As the proposed approach mainly relies on a Monte-Carlo estimator for generating the smoothing parameters. Variance-reduction mechanism and sensitivity analysis are also incorporated into the proposed framework to ensure the robustness of the approximate renderer. Experiments on pose estimation and single-view reconstruction are conducted for comparing the performance of the proposed method and previous state-of-the-art approaches.

Main Review:
Pros:

- By introducing the perturbed optimizer to analyzing the non-differentiability of standard rasterization-based rendering pipeline provides new insight into constructing a generic differentiable renderer. The perturbed differentiable rendering can also be implemented on top of existing renderers without major modifications, which makes this approach versatile and a potential performance booster for existing DR techniques.

Cons:

- In terms of technical novelty, it seems to be a direct application of perturbed optimizer to differentiable rendering. Though it requires some efforts (such as the variance reduction and sensitivity analysis) to adapt the existing theory to this specific application, it is not very challenging as there are existing protocols to follow.

- Evaluation-wise, in the task of single-view reconstruction, though the proposed method can achieve comparable performance compared to the state-of-the-art approaches, it still fails to outperform DIB-R in mean accuracy and also outperformed by SoftRas in many categories, such as the Dresser, Car, etc. The 3D pose estimation is only evaluated on a simple cube which cannot provide a very comprehensive measurement of the proposed approach.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a differentiable rendering method that builds connection between the 3D model and 2D perspective appearance. Different from previous methods, the differentiable rendering is formulated into an optimization problem. Two computer vision tasks, including pose estimation and 3D shape from color image, are evaluated with the proposed renderer and compared to SoftRas [22].
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The performance for pose estimation outperforms existing methods.
- The formulation seems novel.

Weakness:
- Could the proposed formulation be applied to other 3D shape representations, e.g. point cloud, implicit? If so, an experiment should be added. If not, then better to mention this is a mesh-specific technic.

- The algorithm seems to be computational costy, e.g. 1 min per image. Would this heavily affect the training efficiency? While comparing to other methods, e.g. SoftRas, the run-time efficiency and hyper-parameters, e.g. number of iteration, training epoch, should be mentioned. Otherwise the comparison may not be on a fair basis.

- Keeping the boundary differentiable is one of the main issues in existing differentiable rendering technics, which is also argued mainly in this paper. However it is not clear how this is improved by the proposed method. From Fig 2 and 4, it seems the boundary area has obvious artifacts. Also some experiments should be performed to highlight the improvements over occlusion boundary.

- The performance on 3D shape prediction does not outperform existing approaches. 

Overall, despite a novel formulation, the improvements over existing approaches are not clear. As shown in the experiment, the propose method does not necessarily produce more accurate rendering or support better for vision tasks. Considering that the proposed tech is also quite slow, the overall advantage over existing approaches is not very clear.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a differentiable rendering method that builds connection between the 3D model and 2D perspective appearance. Different from previous methods, the differentiable rendering is formulated into an optimization problem. Two computer vision tasks, including pose estimation and 3D shape from color image, are evaluated with the proposed renderer and compared to SoftRas [22].

Main Review:
Strength:
- The performance for pose estimation outperforms existing methods.
- The formulation seems novel.

Weakness:
- Could the proposed formulation be applied to other 3D shape representations, e.g. point cloud, implicit? If so, an experiment should be added. If not, then better to mention this is a mesh-specific technic.

- The algorithm seems to be computational costy, e.g. 1 min per image. Would this heavily affect the training efficiency? While comparing to other methods, e.g. SoftRas, the run-time efficiency and hyper-parameters, e.g. number of iteration, training epoch, should be mentioned. Otherwise the comparison may not be on a fair basis.

- Keeping the boundary differentiable is one of the main issues in existing differentiable rendering technics, which is also argued mainly in this paper. However it is not clear how this is improved by the proposed method. From Fig 2 and 4, it seems the boundary area has obvious artifacts. Also some experiments should be performed to highlight the improvements over occlusion boundary.

- The performance on 3D shape prediction does not outperform existing approaches. 

Overall, despite a novel formulation, the improvements over existing approaches are not clear. As shown in the experiment, the propose method does not necessarily produce more accurate rendering or support better for vision tasks. Considering that the proposed tech is also quite slow, the overall advantage over existing approaches is not very clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No comment.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper presents a novel optimization-based differentiable rendering formulation and demonstrates strong results on pose estimation. While the conceptual contribution appears original and promising, its broader impact is limited by unclear applicability beyond meshes, computational inefficiency, unresolved boundary artifacts, and the absence of fair efficiency comparisons. Additionally, the method does not improve over existing approaches on 3D shape prediction, which undermines the case for its overall advantages.

Strengths
- The formulation appears novel.
- The pose estimation results are strong and outperform existing methods.

Weaknesses
- Generality is unclear: it is not evident whether the approach extends beyond mesh-based rendering to other 3D representations such as point clouds or implicit surfaces.
- Computational cost is high (approximately one minute per image), raising concerns about training efficiency.
- Fair runtime comparisons are missing. In particular, the comparison to SoftRas lacks details on run-time efficiency, number of iterations, and training epochs, making it difficult to assess practical advantages.
- Boundary handling is not convincingly improved. Visible artifacts persist near occlusion boundaries (notably in Figures 2 and 4), calling into question the claimed improvements in boundary differentiability.
- 3D shape prediction performance does not surpass existing approaches.
- Overall, the method’s advantages over existing techniques remain unclear, especially given its slower runtime and lack of superior rendering accuracy or downstream benefits.

Detailed comments
- The strong pose estimation results are noteworthy, but without clearer evidence of efficiency and scalability, it is hard to recommend the approach for broader adoption. The reported per-image runtime is particularly concerning for training regimes that require many iterations.
- The lack of a fair and transparent runtime comparison with SoftRas (including hyper-parameters such as the number of iterations and training epochs) prevents a meaningful assessment of efficiency and practical trade-offs.
- The boundary artifacts near occlusions suggest that the proposed improvements to differentiability at edges and silhouettes are not yet realized in practice. Because occlusion boundaries are a key challenge in differentiable rendering, targeted evaluations are necessary to validate progress.
- On 3D shape prediction from color images, the method does not outperform existing approaches. This weakens the claim that the new formulation leads to better reconstructions or provides tangible benefits for vision tasks.

Suggestions for improvement
- Demonstrate applicability to other 3D shape representations (e.g., point clouds or implicit surfaces) with empirical results; otherwise, clearly state the method is mesh-specific.
- Provide detailed and fair runtime comparisons to baselines such as SoftRas, including run-time efficiency and complete hyper-parameter settings (number of iterations and training epochs).
- Clarify how the method improves boundary differentiability and include focused experiments on occlusion boundaries to quantify improvements and reduce artifacts.
- Improve the 3D shape prediction results or discuss the trade-offs that might justify weaker performance (e.g., gains in stability or other metrics).
- Offer stronger evidence that the method delivers more accurate rendering or better supports downstream vision tasks despite its computational cost.

Conclusion and recommendation
The work introduces a novel differentiable rendering formulation and achieves strong pose estimation results, but the lack of generality, efficiency, and convincing boundary handling, coupled with underwhelming shape prediction performance and incomplete runtime comparisons, limits its contribution at this stage. I recommend rejection.

Rating: 4 — Ok but not good enough (rejection)

title: NAS-Bench-x11 and the Power of Learning Curves

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes another benchmark dataset for NAS, NAS-Bench-x11. Compared to the original benchmarks NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301 and NAS-Bench-NLP, NAS-Bench-x11 contains the full training
information for each candidate architecture especially the learning curves so that it allows multi-fidelity NAS algorithms

NAS-Bench-x11 is a surrogate benchmark, which trains a surrogate model to predict the learning curve of the vast amount of candidate architectures. Prediction of the learning curve can be accomplished by singular value decomposition and noise modeling.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

The paper is well motivated and clearly written. The paper is clear about limitations too.

I think this paper will be valuable to the community.  The created NAS-bench-x11 contains the full training information of different architectures, which may make it more convenient to analyse NAS algorithms and promote further NAS algorithm design. The idea of predicting the learning curve is very interesting to me. 

The authors conducted extensive experiments to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.  

Weakness:

This paper is a completion of the previously created benchmarks. Providing learning curves is also considered before in NAS-Bench-201, which weakens the novelty of this paper. So I recommend a weak acceptence.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents NAS-Bench-x11, a surrogate-based benchmark that provides full training information for many candidate architectures, with a focus on predicting entire learning curves. The approach models learning curves via singular value decomposition combined with noise modeling to generate realistic trajectories. The paper is clearly written, well motivated, and transparent about its limitations and societal impact. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the surrogate is effective and that the resulting benchmark can enable multi-fidelity NAS algorithms and facilitate analysis and design based on learning dynamics.

Strengths
- Clear motivation, strong writing, and thoughtful discussion of limitations and societal impact.
- The benchmark includes complete training information, especially learning curves, which is valuable for multi-fidelity NAS and for deeper analysis of training behavior.
- The central idea of predicting learning curves is interesting and practically useful.
- The methodology—SVD-based learning curve representation with noise modeling—appears sound.
- Experiments are extensive and support the effectiveness of the surrogate benchmark.
- The resource is likely to be useful to the NAS community.

Weaknesses
- The novelty is limited: learning curves have already been provided in NAS-Bench-201, making this work feel more like a completion or extension of prior benchmarks than a fundamentally new contribution.

Overall Assessment
Despite the limited novelty relative to NAS-Bench-201, the paper delivers a clearly presented, empirically solid benchmark with practical utility. The inclusion of full training information and effective learning-curve prediction supports multi-fidelity NAS research and broader analysis, which will likely benefit the community. I recommend weak acceptance.

Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary: Existing NAS benchmarks often only include the architecture performance at the final or some selected checkpoint epochs. However, per-epoch information is essential for developing and evaluating multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. This work proposed to fit a surrogate model to predict the learning curve given an architecture encoding. Utilizing the proposed predictor, the author extends three existing benchmarks NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP to include per-epoch accuracies. Further, to demonstrate the necessity of developing multi-fidelity algorithms for NAS, the author also describes a procedure to extend the single-fidelity algorithms to multi-fidelity ones by using learning curve extrapolation to filter out candidate proposals with partial training. Despite possible room for improvements on the performance of the learning curve predictor used, the proposed method would enable researchers to develop and rapidly test multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. I vote for acceptance.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The contribution of this paper is relevant to the NAS community and would accelerate research on multi-fidelity NAS algorithms.
2. The performance of the proposed learning curve predictor model is overall satisfactory.
3. The author adopted the proposed method to extend three existing benchmarks to include per-epoch information.

Weakness:
1. The author reported ranking correlation and KL divergence between the predicted learning curve and ground truth. The numbers seem reasonable, but the errors are still non-negligible. It is unclear how misleading these errors would be if someone tries to develop multi-fidelity algorithms by querying the proposed extended benchmark. How would the existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms perform under the proposed benchmark, over ground-truth data? This might be doable for NASBench201 or NASBench111 (the version created without using information about the given 4 checkpoint epochs), as they provide ground-truth accuracies on at least some checkpoints?

Comments and Questions:
1. For NASBench-NLP, using the accuracy from the first few epochs as features significantly improves the learning curve predictor's performance. Can we also expect visible improvements on other spaces, if the info of the first few epochs is also provided?

2. You mentioned in para 355 that future surrogate works should save full learning curve info to avoid the need for any extra extensions. But surrogate benchmarks like NASBench-301 only train a small portion of the architectures fully, and the large majority part of the search space will be benched by the predictor. So it seems that such surrogate benchmark would still need to be extended with learning curve prediction anyway. Could you elaborate more on this?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

Summary:
Existing NAS benchmarks often only include the architecture performance at the final or some selected checkpoint epochs. However, per-epoch information is essential for developing and evaluating multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. This work proposed to fit a surrogate model to predict the learning curve given an architecture encoding. Utilizing the proposed predictor, the author extends three existing benchmarks NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP to include per-epoch accuracies. Further, to demonstrate the necessity of developing multi-fidelity algorithms for NAS, the author also describes a procedure to extend the single-fidelity algorithms to multi-fidelity ones by using learning curve extrapolation to filter out candidate proposals with partial training. Despite possible room for improvements on the performance of the learning curve predictor used, the proposed method would enable researchers to develop and rapidly test multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. I vote for acceptance.

Main Review:
Strength:
1. The contribution of this paper is relevant to the NAS community and would accelerate research on multi-fidelity NAS algorithms.
2. The performance of the proposed learning curve predictor model is overall satisfactory.
3. The author adopted the proposed method to extend three existing benchmarks to include per-epoch information.

Weakness:
1. The author reported ranking correlation and KL divergence between the predicted learning curve and ground truth. The numbers seem reasonable, but the errors are still non-negligible. It is unclear how misleading these errors would be if someone tries to develop multi-fidelity algorithms by querying the proposed extended benchmark. How would the existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms perform under the proposed benchmark, over ground-truth data? This might be doable for NASBench201 or NASBench111 (the version created without using information about the given 4 checkpoint epochs), as they provide ground-truth accuracies on at least some checkpoints?

Comments and Questions:
1. For NASBench-NLP, using the accuracy from the first few epochs as features significantly improves the learning curve predictor's performance. Can we also expect visible improvements on other spaces, if the info of the first few epochs is also provided?

2. You mentioned in para 355 that future surrogate works should save full learning curve info to avoid the need for any extra extensions. But surrogate benchmarks like NASBench-301 only train a small portion of the architectures fully, and the large majority part of the search space will be benched by the predictor. So it seems that such surrogate benchmark would still need to be extended with learning curve prediction anyway. Could you elaborate more on this?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents an extension of prominent NAS benchmarks to include per-epoch information via a learning-curve surrogate, thereby enabling and accelerating research on multi-fidelity NAS. The authors extend NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP and report predictor performance using standard metrics (rank correlation and KL divergence). Overall, the contribution is timely and relevant, and the reported predictive performance is generally satisfactory. However, the evaluation leaves open important questions about how the surrogate’s non-negligible errors might affect downstream algorithm development and how claims about saving full learning curves should be interpreted. Despite these reservations, I find the work solid and recommend acceptance.

Strengths
- Clear and relevant contribution to the NAS community: by providing per-epoch information through a learning-curve surrogate, the work directly supports and accelerates research on multi-fidelity NAS algorithms.
- The learning-curve predictor achieves overall satisfactory performance across the examined benchmarks according to rank correlation and KL divergence.
- The paper delivers a tangible resource by extending three widely used benchmarks—NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP—to include per-epoch information, which is essential for multi-fidelity evaluation and candidate filtering after partial training.

Weaknesses and concerns
- The prediction errors (in both rank correlation and KL divergence) are non-negligible. It remains unclear how much these errors could mislead the development or evaluation of multi-fidelity NAS algorithms when researchers rely on the surrogate benchmarks.
- The paper does not quantify the performance gap between existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms evaluated on the proposed surrogate benchmarks and their performance on ground-truth data. This missing piece makes it difficult to assess the practical impact of the surrogate’s inaccuracies.
- The manuscript suggests that future surrogate works should save full learning curve information. Given that surrogate benchmarks such as NASBench-301 fully train only a subset of architectures and must predict the remainder, the justification and implications of this statement need clarification.

Suggestions for improvement
- Evaluate several established multi-fidelity NAS algorithms on the proposed extended benchmarks and compare the results against ground-truth evaluations to quantify the effect of surrogate errors. Suitable ground-truth checks could include NASBench-201 or NASBench-111; for the latter, ensure that comparisons do not use the four checkpoint epochs as additional information during evaluation to avoid leakage.
- For the NASBench-NLP result where providing the first few epochs as features substantially improves prediction, test whether this strategy carries over to other search spaces. This would help determine whether early-epoch features provide a generalizable boost or are specific to the NLP setting.
- Clarify the recommendation that future surrogate works should save full learning curve information. In scenarios where only a subset of architectures is fully trained (as in NASBench-301), most learning curves would still be predicted. Please explain how the proposed practice would be implemented in such cases and what benefits it would bring relative to current approaches.

Additional comments
- The use of per-epoch accuracies and learning-curve extrapolation to filter candidates after partial training is a valuable capability enabled by this work.
- The mention of the four checkpoint epochs available in NASBench-101 is helpful context; please ensure their role is clearly delineated when used as features or held out in comparisons intended to mirror pure surrogate usage.

Decision
7: Good paper, accept. The resource is valuable and likely to accelerate multi-fidelity NAS research. Addressing the concerns above—especially a direct comparison of algorithm performance on surrogate versus ground-truth data and clarifying the guidance on saving full learning curves—would further strengthen the paper.

Comments and Questions:
1. For NASBench-NLP, using the accuracy from the first few epochs as features significantly improves the learning curve predictor's performance. Can we also expect visible improvements on other spaces, if the info of the first few epochs is also provided?

2. You mentioned in para 355 that future surrogate works should save full learning curve info to avoid the need for any extra extensions. But surrogate benchmarks like NASBench-301 only train a small portion of the architectures fully, and the large majority part of the search space will be benched by the predictor. So it seems that such surrogate benchmark would still need to be extended with learning curve prediction anyway. Could you elaborate more on this?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

extracted_ideas.summary: Positive review recommending acceptance. Commends the relevance and utility of extending NAS benchmarks with per-epoch information via a learning-curve surrogate, while noting non-negligible prediction errors and asking for clarity on their impact and on claims about saving full learning curves.
strengths: Contribution is relevant to the NAS community and will accelerate research on multi-fidelity NAS algorithms.
Learning curve predictor performance is overall satisfactory.
Successfully extends three existing benchmarks (NASBench-101, NASBench-301, NASBench-NLP) to include per-epoch information.
weaknesses: Prediction errors (rank correlation and KL divergence) are non-negligible, raising concerns about how misleading they might be when developing multi-fidelity algorithms using the surrogate benchmarks.
Unclear performance gap between existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms evaluated on the proposed surrogate benchmarks versus ground-truth data.
suggestions: Evaluate existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms on the proposed extended benchmarks and compare their performance to evaluations on ground-truth data to quantify the impact of surrogate errors.
For NASBench-NLP-style improvements, test whether providing the first few epochs as features also improves predictors on other search spaces.

Clarify the statement that future surrogate works should save full learning curve information, given that surrogate benchmarks (e.g., NASBench-301) train only a subset fully and still require prediction for the rest.
main_points: The work enables and accelerates research on multi-fidelity NAS by adding per-epoch information via a surrogate learning curve predictor.
The predictor’s accuracy is reasonable but not perfect, and its errors could potentially mislead algorithm development.
A key missing evaluation is how multi-fidelity methods perform on the surrogate versus on ground-truth benchmarks.
Using early-epoch accuracies as features substantially improves prediction in NASBench-NLP; reviewer queries generalization to other spaces.
Questioning the claim about saving full learning curves in future surrogates, since most architectures in surrogates are not fully trained and would still need predicted learning curves.

Benchmarks discussed: NASBench-101, NASBench-301, NASBench-NLP; suggestion to compare on NASBench-201 or NASBench-111 (without using the four checkpoint epochs) for ground-truth checks.
Use of per-epoch accuracies and learning curve extrapolation to filter candidates after partial training.
Noted improvement on NASBench-NLP when using first few epochs as features; mention of four checkpoint epochs available in NASBench-101.

reviews.summary: The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.
reviews.main_review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

Summary:
The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.

Main Review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
The paper presents surrogate benchmarks for NAS, exploring multiple approaches to learning-curve embeddings, prediction from architecture representations, and noise modeling. The methodology and alternative design choices are clearly described, and empirical evidence is provided to justify decisions. The paper also includes a study on learning curve extrapolation (LCE) that serves as a useful reminder to the community about potential speed-ups, and it commendably discusses limitations and societal impact.

Strengths
- The method and its design alternatives are clearly and carefully laid out.
- Empirical results generally support the methodological choices.
- The inclusion of an LCE study is a helpful reminder for the NAS community regarding efficiency gains.
- The authors are transparent about limitations and consider societal impact.

Weaknesses
- The practical usefulness of the proposed surrogate benchmarks is unclear due to issues in the noise modeling and resulting artifacts. The current noise modeling is treated as architecture- and hyperparameter-independent, which seems implausible in practice.
- Predicted learning curves exhibit artifacts not observed in real training, notably early-epoch values that exceed the maximum of the overall curve (as illustrated in Figure 1), suggesting the injected or modeled noise is too high.
- These issues indicate that the surrogates may embed biases that deviate from reality, making them more akin to synthetic datasets. As a result, they appear unreliable for evaluating NAS methods; at best, they may be suitable for prototyping.
- The LCE study, while useful as a reminder, does not provide novelty in the NAS context, given that such techniques have already been used in prior work.

Suggestions for improvement
- Clarify and quantify how the identified issues in the surrogate benchmarks affect downstream NAS evaluations. Concrete analyses of failure modes and their impact on rankings or selection would help assess practical utility.
- Improve the noise modeling to account for dependence on architectures and hyperparameters, reducing reliance on unrealistic independence assumptions.
- Address artifacts such as early-epoch predictions exceeding the curve’s maximum by mitigating excessive noise and ensuring consistency with real learning-curve dynamics.
- Position the LCE results more carefully relative to prior NAS work and clearly delineate what, if anything, is novel in the current setup.

Overall assessment
This work is clearly written and empirically grounded, with thoughtful consideration of design choices and limitations. However, the core contribution—surrogate benchmarks for NAS—suffers from noise modeling assumptions that lead to unrealistic artifacts and potential biases, undermining confidence in their use for NAS evaluation. Combined with the limited novelty of the LCE study in the NAS context, the paper falls short of the bar for acceptance at this time.

Rating: 5 — Marginally below the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary: Existing NAS benchmark datasets (e.g., NAS-Bench-101) do not provide full training information of architectures, which make it infeasible to evaluate the multi-fidelity algorithms (e.g., learning curve extrapolation). To address these issues, this paper create surrogate benchmark datasets, namely NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311 and NAS-Bench-NLP11, with singular value decomposition and noise modeling methods. Extensive experimental results with the full training information on these datasets show the superiority of the proposed method.
reviews.main_review:
## Positive points:
1. The paper presents a method to extend the existing single-fidelity benchmarks to multi-fidelity ones with full training information.
2. The authors create NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11 benchmark datasets, which may be beneficial to develop new NAS algorithm.

## Negative points:
1. Does the proposed method apply to real world NAS application? In practice, given a dataset, we may have no available training information of any architectures. In this case, how to apply the proposed method? It would be limited if the proposed method is only able to extend the existing NAS benchmarks.
2. In line 220, why do the authors sample architectures from BANANAS, local search and regularized evolution?
3. In line 225, the authors mention that the method is able to improve the performance with the accuracies from the tabular benchmark (at epochs 4, 12, 36, 108) as additional features. More details of this should be provided.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
Existing NAS benchmark datasets (e.g., NAS-Bench-101) do not provide full training information of architectures, which make it infeasible to evaluate the multi-fidelity algorithms (e.g., learning curve extrapolation). To address these issues, this paper create surrogate benchmark datasets, namely NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311 and NAS-Bench-NLP11, with singular value decomposition and noise modeling methods. Extensive experimental results with the full training information on these datasets show the superiority of the proposed method.

Main Review:
## Positive points:
1. The paper presents a method to extend the existing single-fidelity benchmarks to multi-fidelity ones with full training information.
2. The authors create NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11 benchmark datasets, which may be beneficial to develop new NAS algorithm.

## Negative points:
1. Does the proposed method apply to real world NAS application? In practice, given a dataset, we may have no available training information of any architectures. In this case, how to apply the proposed method? It would be limited if the proposed method is only able to extend the existing NAS benchmarks.
2. In line 220, why do the authors sample architectures from BANANAS, local search and regularized evolution?
3. In line 225, the authors mention that the method is able to improve the performance with the accuracies from the tabular benchmark (at epochs 4, 12, 36, 108) as additional features. More details of this should be provided.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper proposes a principled approach to constructing surrogate NAS benchmarks with full learning-curve information by combining singular value decomposition (SVD) with noise modeling. The method extends single-fidelity tabular benchmarks to multi-fidelity ones, enabling learning-curve extrapolation and evaluation across budgets. The authors instantiate this framework to produce NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11, and report extensive experiments showing favorable performance of the proposed surrogates. The paper also explicitly discusses limitations and potential societal impact.

Strengths
- Clear and useful contribution: a method to augment existing single-fidelity benchmarks into multi-fidelity surrogates with complete training information, enabling richer evaluation protocols.
- Tangible artifacts: the release of NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11 could catalyze research on multi-fidelity NAS and learning-curve-aware optimization.
- Strong empirical support: the experiments are extensive and suggest the proposed approach outperforms alternatives.
- Thoughtful presentation: the manuscript acknowledges limitations and touches on societal considerations.

Weaknesses and points needing clarification
- Applicability to real-world NAS: It remains unclear how the approach would be used when no training information is available for any architecture—a common practical scenario. As written, the method appears primarily suited to extending existing tabular benchmarks rather than guiding NAS in settings without prior training data.
- Sampling strategy (line 220): The choice to sample architectures using BANANAS, local search, and regularized evolution is not sufficiently justified. The rationale for these particular sources, and whether they bias the surrogate or affect generalization, needs clarification.
- Feature augmentation (line 225): The description of using tabular benchmark accuracies at epochs 4, 12, 36, and 108 as additional features is underspecified. It is not clear how these values are integrated into the model inputs and what empirical impact they have on surrogate accuracy or stability.

Suggestions for improvement
- Real-world applicability: Please articulate how the method could be deployed when no training information exists for any architecture. For example, what minimal data would be required to initialize the SVD/noise model, and how would one proceed in a cold-start setting? If the approach is intended only for extending existing benchmarks, state this explicitly and discuss implications.
- Sampling rationale (line 220): Provide a clear justification for selecting BANANAS, local search, and regularized evolution as sampling mechanisms. Explain whether these methods approximate diverse regions of the search space, and discuss any impact on the learned surrogate’s bias or coverage.
- Feature integration and effects (line 225): Detail how accuracies at epochs 4, 12, 36, and 108 are incorporated (e.g., concatenation to architecture encodings, normalization, handling of missing values) and report their empirical effect on performance. A brief ablation or quantitative comparison would help establish their contribution.

Overall assessment
The paper offers a timely and useful contribution by turning single-fidelity NAS benchmarks into multi-fidelity surrogates with full learning curves using SVD and noise modeling, and the new benchmarks have potential to benefit the community. The experimental results are compelling. However, important questions remain about the method’s applicability in real-world NAS without existing training data and about specific design choices (sampling and feature augmentation). Given these unresolved issues, I recommend a rating of 5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold. With the above clarifications, the work could be significantly strengthened.

title: Compressed Video Contrastive Learning

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a self-supervised method for compressed video representation learning, which helps save both storage and computation.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

- The proposed method directly uses RGB and motion vectors in the compressed video for efficient representation learning.
- Experimental results show that the proposed method reduces the total (inference) time and storage, and achieves marginal improvement compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Weakness:

- Adding temporal learning objectives such as changing the temporal order of RGB frames or motion vectors within a clip ( as in IMRNet ), which is missing from the proposed method, would greatly help learning video representation.
- Modeling of the proposed method mostly applies the InfoNCE loss, only selecting of positive and negative samples are updated for RGB and motion vectors.
- As shown in table 1, the computation overhead largely comes from the preprocessing time. Though not an apple-to-apple comparison, parallel re-encoding for IMRNet may largely mitigate the overhead?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The reviewer would encourage the authors to discuss limitations and impacts of the proposed method, as mentioned in the 'Main Review'.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a self-supervised method for compressed video representation learning, which helps save both storage and computation.

Main Review:
Strength:

- The proposed method directly uses RGB and motion vectors in the compressed video for efficient representation learning.
- Experimental results show that the proposed method reduces the total (inference) time and storage, and achieves marginal improvement compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Weakness:

- Adding temporal learning objectives such as changing the temporal order of RGB frames or motion vectors within a clip ( as in IMRNet ), which is missing from the proposed method, would greatly help learning video representation.
- Modeling of the proposed method mostly applies the InfoNCE loss, only selecting of positive and negative samples are updated for RGB and motion vectors.
- As shown in table 1, the computation overhead largely comes from the preprocessing time. Though not an apple-to-apple comparison, parallel re-encoding for IMRNet may largely mitigate the overhead?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The reviewer would encourage the authors to discuss limitations and impacts of the proposed method, as mentioned in the 'Main Review'.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper proposes a self-supervised video representation learning approach that directly leverages RGB frames and motion vectors available in compressed videos. The method operates within an InfoNCE framework, with the main modeling change being how positive and negative samples are selected across the RGB and motion-vector views. Empirically, the approach reduces storage and both total/inference time relative to baselines, while yielding marginal accuracy improvements over state-of-the-art methods.

Strengths
- Practical efficiency: By exploiting RGB and motion vectors from the compressed domain, the method offers a resource-efficient pipeline for video representation learning.
- Measured gains in efficiency: The experiments report reductions in storage and total/inference time, which is valuable for large-scale or deployment-oriented settings.
- Competitive performance: Despite being modest, the accuracy gains over strong baselines indicate the utility of the design.

Weaknesses
- Limited temporal modeling: The method does not include temporal learning objectives (e.g., altering temporal order within clips as in IMRNet), which could substantially enhance representation learning for video.
- Constrained novelty: Beyond adjusting positive/negative sample selection within an InfoNCE loss, the modeling contributions appear limited.
- Questionable fairness in efficiency comparisons: The efficiency analysis indicates that preprocessing dominates computation overhead (as shown in Table 1). Given this, the comparison with IMRNet may not be apples-to-apples; IMRNet’s preprocessing overhead could be reduced with parallel re-encoding, potentially narrowing the reported efficiency gap.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Incorporate temporal learning objectives: Integrating temporal order manipulation for both RGB frames and motion vectors within a clip could strengthen the learned representations.
- Clarify and equalize efficiency comparisons: Either evaluate a version of IMRNet with parallel re-encoding or explicitly discuss how preprocessing choices affect the reported results to ensure a fair comparison.
- Add a limitations and societal impact discussion: A dedicated section would help contextualize the method’s scope, potential failure modes, and broader implications.

Overall Assessment
The work presents an appealing, efficient approach to learning from compressed videos and demonstrates tangible efficiency benefits with marginal accuracy gains over state-of-the-art methods. However, the absence of temporal objectives and the limited modeling novelty temper the contribution, and the claims around efficiency would be stronger with more balanced preprocessing comparisons. On balance, I find the practical relevance and empirical efficiency gains sufficient to merit publication, while encouraging the authors to address the concerns above.

Recommendation: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary: The authors propose a self-supervised learning (SSL) method for learning strong visual representations based on compressed videos. Compared with prior work, which sees videos in RGB only, the proposed method is more efficient and leverages the motion information that's already accessible in compressed videos. The authors propose to use a contrastive learning method to supervise on kind of modality with correspondence of another modality. On multiple datasets (mainly UCF and HMDB), good performance is achieved, with both linear probing and transfer learning settings.
reviews.main_review:
[originality: medium-high]
 - The idea of using compressed videos for self-supervised learning is novel/original. Most prior work that operates on compressed videos focues on supervised learning setting only. The implementation of this idea is standard/expected (this is not a drawback).

[quality: medium-high]
 - The paper covers useful ablation study that I'd expect. In particular, the authors demonstrate the usefulness of the compressed-video modality (MV) compared to RGB only (Table 3). Compared to prior work, the performance is also strong while being significantly more efficient. The ablation study that compares with optical flow and low-res optical flow is also interesting (Table 2).

[clarity: high]
 - I think this paper is overall clearly writen. One question about implementation details: How is the similarity calculated in the hard-positive mining procedure?

[significance: medium-high]
 - The area of SSL draws a lot of attention in our community and is of great potential. I think this work extends our knowledge from a new dimension with the use of compressed videos. I think this work can lead to promising new directions in SSL research.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1. In the paper, the authors perform experiments/evaluation solely on videos. I was wondering if the method learns good representations for "image" tasks as well. Namely, leveraging MV can potentially give us better RGB representations for images, too. I wonder what the authors think about this.
2. If the hard-positive mining is based on the learned features, would there be any "chichen-and-egg" problem introduced by the proposed procedure?
3. I like the comparison to MV_{CoViAR}, but I wonder how each specific compression setting affects the results. For example, how does GOP size, the use of B-frame vs P-frame, etc. affect results.

Regarding societal impact, I don't see anything particularly concerning (or more concerning) than other SSL work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors propose a self-supervised learning (SSL) method for learning strong visual representations based on compressed videos. Compared with prior work, which sees videos in RGB only, the proposed method is more efficient and leverages the motion information that's already accessible in compressed videos. The authors propose to use a contrastive learning method to supervise on kind of modality with correspondence of another modality. On multiple datasets (mainly UCF and HMDB), good performance is achieved, with both linear probing and transfer learning settings.

Main Review:
[originality: medium-high]
 - The idea of using compressed videos for self-supervised learning is novel/original. Most prior work that operates on compressed videos focues on supervised learning setting only. The implementation of this idea is standard/expected (this is not a drawback).

[quality: medium-high]
 - The paper covers useful ablation study that I'd expect. In particular, the authors demonstrate the usefulness of the compressed-video modality (MV) compared to RGB only (Table 3). Compared to prior work, the performance is also strong while being significantly more efficient. The ablation study that compares with optical flow and low-res optical flow is also interesting (Table 2).

[clarity: high]
 - I think this paper is overall clearly writen. One question about implementation details: How is the similarity calculated in the hard-positive mining procedure?

[significance: medium-high]
 - The area of SSL draws a lot of attention in our community and is of great potential. I think this work extends our knowledge from a new dimension with the use of compressed videos. I think this work can lead to promising new directions in SSL research.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
1. In the paper, the authors perform experiments/evaluation solely on videos. I was wondering if the method learns good representations for "image" tasks as well. Namely, leveraging MV can potentially give us better RGB representations for images, too. I wonder what the authors think about this.
2. If the hard-positive mining is based on the learned features, would there be any "chichen-and-egg" problem introduced by the proposed procedure?
3. I like the comparison to MV_{CoViAR}, but I wonder how each specific compression setting affects the results. For example, how does GOP size, the use of B-frame vs P-frame, etc. affect results.

Regarding societal impact, I don't see anything particularly concerning (or more concerning) than other SSL work.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents a novel and efficient self-supervised learning approach that leverages motion information from compressed videos, specifically motion vectors, rather than relying solely on RGB frames. The method is clearly described and achieves strong results on UCF and HMDB under both linear probing and transfer settings. Ablations substantiate the benefits of exploiting motion vectors over RGB-only baselines and provide informative comparisons to optical flow and low-resolution optical flow.

Strengths
- Originality: Applying compressed video motion information to self-supervised learning is a compelling and timely idea, extending prior use of such signals beyond supervised settings.
- Efficiency: The approach is more computationally efficient than RGB-only methods while effectively leveraging motion cues readily available in compressed video.
- Empirical performance: Results on UCF and HMDB are strong across linear probing and transfer learning, indicating that the learned representations are useful.
- Ablations: The paper includes useful analyses, including MV vs. RGB (Table 3) and optical flow vs. low-resolution optical flow (Table 2), and a comparison to MV_CoViAR.
- Presentation: The paper is clearly written and easy to follow.

Weaknesses and Questions
- Scope of evaluation: Experiments are limited to video tasks. It remains unclear whether the learned representations transfer to image tasks, which would speak to broader applicability.
- Hard-positive mining details: The implementation details for the hard-positive mining step are incomplete. In particular, the similarity metric used to identify hard positives is not specified.
- Potential circularity: If the hard-positive selection relies on features learned by the model, there is a potential chicken-and-egg concern that merits discussion.
- Sensitivity to compression: The paper does not analyze how performance depends on compression settings, such as GOP size or the use of B- vs. P-frames. Similarly, while the comparison to MV_CoViAR is appreciated, a broader examination across different compression configurations would strengthen the claims.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Clarify the hard-positive mining procedure by explicitly stating how similarity is computed.
- Discuss whether the reliance on learned features in hard-positive mining introduces a chicken-and-egg issue and how this is mitigated in practice.
- Evaluate or at least comment on the transfer of the learned representations to image tasks to better assess generalizability.
- Analyze the impact of different compression settings (e.g., GOP length, inclusion of B- vs. P-frames) on performance, and extend the MV_CoViAR comparison across multiple compression configurations.

Societal Impact
The societal implications appear similar to other self-supervised learning work; nothing stands out as particularly concerning in this regard.

Recommendation
7: Good paper, accept. The method is original and efficient, the empirical results and ablations are convincing, and the paper is clearly written. Addressing the questions around hard-positive mining and the sensitivity to compression parameters, as well as commenting on transfer to image tasks, would further strengthen the work.

reviews.summary: The paper tackles the problem of self-supervised video representation learning. The authors propose a compressed video based self-supervised learning approach, where the model is forced to learn matching representations between raw RGB clip and its corresponding motion vector clip. This is achieved using a relatively standard contrastive learning formulation. The authors then demonstrate that their model achieves state-of-the-art performance on a few downstream tasks (i.e., action recognition, and video retrieval) while also being significantly more efficient (particularly in terms of the video processing time).
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
- The paper is written clearly, and it addresses a well motivated and an important research problem.
- A nice idea of using compressed video for self-supervised feature learning.
- The proposed approach delivers performance that is comparable/better to previous methods (e.g. CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]) while being much more efficient.
- Thorough ablation studies.

Weaknesses:
- The major weakness of this paper is its limited technical novelty. While the idea of using compressed videos in the context of self-supervised learning is new and interesting, overall the proposed approach is largely built on the ideas from CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]. Having said this, the proposed approach seems to be both more effective and more efficient than CoCLR. Therefore, I think this would be a good "poster" paper.
- The proposed approach performs quite a bit worse than the recent CVPR 2021 methods such as CVRL, and especially the method of Feichtenhofer et al. "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning" (CVPR 2021). Having said this, given a very large computational cost of both of these prior methods (i.e. 800 epoch training), I think that it would be unfair to compare the proposed MVCGC approach directly with these methods. The authors should still add the second reference to their related work section, and also to their quantitative comparisons. A discussion on why the direct comparison between these methods is unfair would also be useful.

Questions / Minor Details:
- In L85-88, the authors mention that unlike CoCLR, they construct sample pairs between different views of different clip (i.e., RGB frames of one clip and motion vectors of another clip from the same video). However, the caption of Fig.3 says that the motion vector clip corresponds to the raw RGB clip. Therefore, I was confused, which one is it? Also, if it's the former, could the authors also ablate the latter?
- Typo in Table 3 (it should be Retrieval instead of Retrival). 
- In the third part of Table 4, the authors highlight/bold the numbers corresponding to their approach even though CVRL achieves higher accuracy.
- If the authors could include the linear evaluation on Kinetics-400 (similar to "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning") that would be great. I think the evaluation on UCF and HMDB is a bit saturated and outdated. Therefore, it would be useful to have additional numbers on Kinetics, which is a bit more challenging dataset.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations were not discussed.

Summary:
The paper tackles the problem of self-supervised video representation learning. The authors propose a compressed video based self-supervised learning approach, where the model is forced to learn matching representations between raw RGB clip and its corresponding motion vector clip. This is achieved using a relatively standard contrastive learning formulation. The authors then demonstrate that their model achieves state-of-the-art performance on a few downstream tasks (i.e., action recognition, and video retrieval) while also being significantly more efficient (particularly in terms of the video processing time).

Main Review:
Strengths:
- The paper is written clearly, and it addresses a well motivated and an important research problem.
- A nice idea of using compressed video for self-supervised feature learning.
- The proposed approach delivers performance that is comparable/better to previous methods (e.g. CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]) while being much more efficient.
- Thorough ablation studies.

Weaknesses:
- The major weakness of this paper is its limited technical novelty. While the idea of using compressed videos in the context of self-supervised learning is new and interesting, overall the proposed approach is largely built on the ideas from CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]. Having said this, the proposed approach seems to be both more effective and more efficient than CoCLR. Therefore, I think this would be a good "poster" paper.
- The proposed approach performs quite a bit worse than the recent CVPR 2021 methods such as CVRL, and especially the method of Feichtenhofer et al. "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning" (CVPR 2021). Having said this, given a very large computational cost of both of these prior methods (i.e. 800 epoch training), I think that it would be unfair to compare the proposed MVCGC approach directly with these methods. The authors should still add the second reference to their related work section, and also to their quantitative comparisons. A discussion on why the direct comparison between these methods is unfair would also be useful.

Questions / Minor Details:
- In L85-88, the authors mention that unlike CoCLR, they construct sample pairs between different views of different clip (i.e., RGB frames of one clip and motion vectors of another clip from the same video). However, the caption of Fig.3 says that the motion vector clip corresponds to the raw RGB clip. Therefore, I was confused, which one is it? Also, if it's the former, could the authors also ablate the latter?
- Typo in Table 3 (it should be Retrieval instead of Retrival). 
- In the third part of Table 4, the authors highlight/bold the numbers corresponding to their approach even though CVRL achieves higher accuracy.
- If the authors could include the linear evaluation on Kinetics-400 (similar to "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning") that would be great. I think the evaluation on UCF and HMDB is a bit saturated and outdated. Therefore, it would be useful to have additional numbers on Kinetics, which is a bit more challenging dataset.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitations were not discussed.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
This paper presents MVCGC, a self-supervised video representation learning approach that leverages compressed video signals. The writing is clear, the problem is well-motivated, and the method is efficient, achieving results comparable to or better than related approaches such as CoCLR while training with substantially lower computational cost. The ablation studies are thorough and help unpack design choices. However, the technical novelty is incremental and largely builds on CoCLR, and the method underperforms recent compute-heavy CVPR 2021 approaches (e.g., CVRL and Feichtenhofer et al.). The paper would benefit from clearer positioning relative to these works, clarification of the sample pairing strategy, expanded evaluation, and some presentation fixes. Overall, I find the contribution practically useful and marginally above the acceptance threshold.

Strengths
- Clear, well-written paper addressing an important and timely problem in video self-supervision.
- Practical and novel use of compressed videos for feature learning, delivering a compelling efficiency–accuracy trade-off.
- Competitive or better performance than prior methods such as CoCLR at significantly lower computational cost.
- Thorough ablation studies that provide insight into the method’s components.

Weaknesses
- Limited technical novelty; the approach is substantially built on CoCLR despite meaningful improvements.
- Lower accuracy than recent CVPR 2021 methods (CVRL; Feichtenhofer et al.) that use much larger computational budgets.
- The paper does not discuss its limitations.

Suggestions for improvement
- Related work and comparisons: Please explicitly include and discuss CVRL and Feichtenhofer et al. (“A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning”). Add quantitative comparisons where possible. At the same time, include a clear discussion of why direct comparison to these compute-heavy methods (e.g., 800-epoch training) is not apples-to-apples, and frame MVCGC’s contribution around efficiency and practicality.
- Clarify positive pair construction: There is an inconsistency between L85–88 (pairs formed from different clips of the same video across views) and the Fig. 3 caption (motion-vector clip corresponds to the same raw RGB clip). Please clarify which strategy is used and, if feasible, provide an ablation comparing “corresponding clips” versus “different clips from the same video” across modalities.
- Broaden evaluation: In addition to UCF/HMDB, please report linear evaluation on Kinetics-400 to provide a more current and challenging benchmark and strengthen the empirical scope.
- Add a limitations and societal impact section: Briefly discuss the method’s limitations (e.g., reliance on compressed streams, potential domain constraints) and any broader impacts.

Presentation and minor issues
- Table 3: Fix the typo “Retrival” to “Retrieval.”
- Table 4: Correct the bold highlighting where MVCGC values are bolded despite CVRL having higher accuracy.

Overall assessment
The paper offers a practical and efficient approach with solid empirical results and careful ablations, though the core novelty is limited and performance lags recent, compute-intensive state-of-the-art methods. With the requested clarifications, expanded comparisons, and presentation fixes, the contribution would be clearer and stronger. I recommend acceptance at the “marginally above threshold” level.

Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)
